_doc: Universal passage-provisioning kit — the gear/spares/stores carried regardless of which boat is chosen. Authored editorial (W3), mission-tuned. The BOAT-SPECIFIC half lives per-boat in research/data/<id>.json -> research.provisioning_specific + scores.provisioning_specific. Keep this list boat-agnostic; anything that depends on a particular engine/rig/system belongs in the boat-specific list.

mission.route: Patagonia & high-latitude (cold, remote, williwaws) -> tropics/Polynesia (heat, UV, long ocean passages)
mission.profile: Offshore passage-making, long autonomy between resupply, sub-$1M budget
implications: Must self-rescue and self-repair: nearest yard can be 1000+ nm away.
Two climate extremes -> carry BOTH cold-weather survival and tropical heat/UV management.
Two dogs aboard add safety, hygiene, medical and stores requirements most lists ignore.
Short-handed -> redundancy in self-steering, reefing, and anchoring so one person can manage everything.

priority_key.essential: Do not leave the dock for an ocean passage without it.
priority_key.recommended: Strongly advised for this route; a few crews skip it and regret it.
priority_key.comfort: Quality-of-life or convenience; carry if budget/space allow.

categories.label: Safety & survival
categories.note: Offshore, remote, two climate extremes. Sized for 4 souls + 2 dogs.

items.item: Offshore liferaft (ISO 9650-1 / SOLAS-B), 4+ person, valise or canister
items.why: Abandon-ship platform; service interval and packing matter more than headline capacity.
items.spec: Add capacity for dogs' weight + grab-bag bulk; carry the dogs in, not towed.

items.item: Ditch bag / grab bag (floating, lashed by companionway)
items.why: Everything you need in the 30 seconds before stepping up to the raft.
items.spec: Contains: handheld GPS, handheld VHF, PLB, flares, water, signalling mirror, whistle, sunblock, space blankets, fishing kit, multitool, copies of passports.

items.item: 406 MHz EPIRB with GPS, registered, auto/manual
items.why: The primary distress alert to SAR from anywhere on the globe.
items.spec: Check battery expiry; register to the vessel with float plan contacts.

items.item: Personal Locator Beacons (PLB) — one per crew
items.why: Person-overboard at night, short-handed, is the highest-probability fatal event.
items.spec: Worn on the PFD, not stowed.

items.item: Inflatable PFDs with integrated harness + spray hood + crotch strap — 1 per crew, plus 1 spare
items.why: Auto-inflate, harness clip-in, and a hood to keep spray out of the airway.
items.spec: Fit AIS-MOB beacon + light + whistle to each; service annually.

items.item: AIS-MOB beacons (one per PFD)
items.why: Puts the casualty's position on your own plotter for a short-handed recovery.

items.item: Jacklines (bow-to-stern, both sides) + cockpit clip points
items.why: Stay attached without unclipping when moving fore-aft.
items.spec: Webbing run inboard so a tether keeps you ON the boat, not dragging alongside.

items.item: Tethers — double, elasticated, with overload-indicator clips (1 per crew + spare)
items.why: Clip-in discipline is the single biggest factor in short-handed offshore safety.

items.item: MOB recovery gear: Lifesling + dedicated halyard/tackle
items.why: A short-handed crew cannot deadlift an exhausted casualty; you need a mechanical-advantage system.
items.spec: Practice the full recovery before departure.

items.item: Danbuoy + horseshoe + floating light + drogue
items.why: Marks the spot and slows your drift away from it.

items.item: SOLAS flare pack + laser flare + orange smoke
items.why: Visual distress, day and night; laser flare avoids expiry/percussive hazards.

items.item: Fire extinguishers (engine bay auto + galley + cabins) + fire blanket
items.why: Engine-room and galley fires are the common causes; auto-discharge unit in the engine space.

items.item: Storm drogue (series drogue) and/or parachute sea anchor + chafe-proof bridle
items.why: Survival tactics for Southern Ocean / high-latitude gales when running or lying-to.
items.spec: Series (Jordan) drogue for running; para-anchor for lying bow-to. Match to displacement.

items.item: Emergency tiller + test-fit
items.why: Steering-failure backup; many crews never test it until it's too late.

items.item: Softwood damage-control plugs (tapered, one per through-hull, tied nearby) + collision mat
items.why: Flooding control after a through-hull or impact failure.

items.item: High-capacity manual bilge pump + spare diaphragms + dewatering pump
items.why: Electrical bilge pumps fail when the batteries flood; you need a manual fallback and a high-volume option.

items.item: Radar reflector / active RTE
items.why: Be seen by ships in fog and at night.

items.item: Night-vision monocular or thermal camera
items.why: Spotting growlers/ice in Patagonia and unlit craft/containers anywhere.

categories.label: Ground tackle & mooring
categories.note: Anchoring is the primary 'parking' in remote cruising; in Patagonia you anchor + shore-tie nightly.

items.item: Primary anchor, oversized new-generation (Rocna/Spade/Mantus class)
items.why: Go one size up from builder spec for high-latitude blows.
items.spec: Sizing is boat-dependent (displacement/windage) -> see boat-specific list for the exact size.

items.item: Calibrated chain rode, full length + spare
items.why: All-chain for catenary and chafe resistance over rock.
items.spec: Mark every 10 m; carry a splice/spare section.

items.item: Secondary (kedge) anchor + mixed rode
items.why: Second anchor for Bahamian moor, fouled-primary backup, or kedging off.

items.item: Storm/third anchor + rode
items.why: When the forecast is genuinely bad and you can't move.

items.item: Snubbers/bridles (2) + chain hook + chafe gear
items.why: Take shock load off the windlass and quiet the boat at anchor.

items.item: Shore-tie kit: 4x long floating lines (60–120 m), rock chocks, ascender, dry bag
items.why: Patagonian caletas require tying to trees/rocks; this kit is non-negotiable there.

items.item: Anchor trip line + float + kellet (anchor weight)
items.why: Retrieve a fouled anchor over rock/kelp; kellet improves holding in gusts.

items.item: Spare windlass parts: gypsy, clutch cones, motor brushes, foot-switch
items.why: A dead windlass short-handed is a serious problem; the wear parts are predictable.
items.spec: Exact gypsy/motor model is boat-specific -> see boat list.

categories.label: Navigation & communications
categories.note: Redundant nav + global comms for weather routing in remote waters.

items.item: Primary chartplotter + fully independent backup (tablet w/ Navionics + OpenCPN on a laptop)
items.why: Single-plotter failure mid-ocean must not blind you.

items.item: Paper charts + pilot books for the route + plotting tools
items.why: Final fallback when all electronics die; pilot books carry local knowledge electronics lack.
items.spec: Patagonia channels (RCC/Italian/Patagonia & Tierra del Fuego Nautical Guide), Pacific pilots.

items.item: Satellite communicator with weather/GRIB + 2-way messaging (Starlink + Iridium GO!/Garmin inReach as backup)
items.why: Weather routing and emergency comms beyond cell/VHF range. Starlink for bandwidth, Iridium for global fallback when Starlink has no coverage/power.

items.item: SSB/HF receiver (or sat) for weatherfax/nets
items.why: High-latitude weather and cruiser nets; receive-only is cheap insurance.

items.item: AIS transceiver (Class B) + handheld VHF/DSC (2, waterproof, floating)
items.why: Collision avoidance + ship comms; handhelds survive a main-set or power failure.

items.item: Fixed VHF/DSC with masthead + emergency antenna
items.why: Primary short-range comms; emergency antenna for a dismasting.

items.item: Hand-bearing compass + handheld GPS (in ditch bag)
items.why: Pilotage and a position fix independent of ship's power.

items.item: Radar (or confirm boat has it) + reflector
items.why: Squalls, shipping, and ice/landfall in poor visibility.

items.item: Barometer (recording) + thermometer/hygrometer
items.why: Local trend forecasting between GRIB downloads.

categories.label: Self-steering, power & water (universal)
categories.note: Short-handed redundancy. Boat-specific autopilot/watermaker models live in the boat list.

items.item: Wind-vane self-steering (mechanical) + spares
items.why: Steers with zero electrical draw on long downwind passages — critical for a short-handed couple and battery autonomy.
items.spec: Vane TYPE is boat-dependent (transom/rudder geometry) -> boat-specific list.

items.item: Redundant autopilot capacity (spare drive or full second pilot)
items.why: Autopilot failure mid-passage forces hand-steering 24/7 with only 2 crew.

items.item: Solar + wind generation already aboard; carry spare charge controller + fuses
items.why: Energy autonomy; controllers are a common silent failure point.

items.item: Portable generator or DC-DC redundancy + fuel
items.why: Charge when solar is weak (Patagonia winters) without running the main engine hard.

items.item: Watermaker spares: membranes, pre-filters, high-pressure pump seals, pickling chemicals
items.why: Extends water autonomy beyond tankage; the consumables are predictable.
items.spec: Unit model is boat-specific; carry one spare membrane set.

items.item: Collapsible jerry cans (water + diesel) + transfer pump + funnel w/ Baja filter
items.why: Top up from shore/dinghy where there's no fuel dock; Baja filter keeps dirty fuel out.

items.item: Battery monitor + spare house-bank fuses/terminals
items.why: Know your true state-of-charge; corroded terminals mimic a dead bank.

categories.label: Universal engine & systems spares
categories.note: Engine-model-specific parts (impellers, belts, injectors) belong in the boat-specific list. These are the truly cross-boat consumables.

items.item: Fuel filters/elements (multiple primary + secondary)
items.why: Stirred-up tank sludge in a seaway clogs filters fast; carry many.

items.item: Assorted hose + double hose clamps (all sizes) + self-fusing silicone tape
items.why: A burst hose or weeping clamp is the most common at-sea repair.

items.item: Sealants & adhesives: marine 3M 4200/5200, butyl tape, epoxy (fast + slow), thread-lock
items.why: Bedding, leak-stopping, and structural repairs without a yard.

items.item: Electrical kit: marine wire (gauges), heat-shrink terminals, crimper, fuses, bulbs/LEDs, dielectric grease
items.why: Corrosion and chafe kill wiring offshore; rebuild a circuit at sea.

items.item: Multimeter + clamp ammeter + circuit tester
items.why: Diagnose electrical faults instead of guessing.

items.item: Rigging spares: bulldog clamps, spare shackles/blocks, soft shackles, Dyneema, cotter/clevis pins, rigging tape, bosun's chair
items.why: Jury-rig a failed fitting and get up the mast at sea.

items.item: Emergency standing-rigging repair kit (Sta-Lok/Norseman fittings, wire, cable cutters/hacksaw)
items.why: Replace a failed shroud or cut away a rig after a dismasting.
items.spec: Wire diameter is boat-specific; cutters must match it.

items.item: Sail repair kit: sailcloth, sticky-back Dacron, palm + needles, waxed thread, webbing, spare batten
items.why: Blown seams and battens are routine on a long passage.

items.item: Assorted fasteners (316 SS) + tap & die set
items.why: Vibration loosens and loses fasteners; chase damaged threads.

items.item: Spare bilge pump + float switches + impellers (general)
items.why: Redundancy on the system that keeps water out.

categories.label: Tools & workshop
categories.note: You are the yard. A short-handed crew still needs to do every job.

items.item: Comprehensive hand-tool set (metric + imperial), corrosion-protected, in a dry roll
items.why: Most boats mix metric and imperial fasteners.

items.item: Cordless drill/driver + bits + spare batteries
items.why: Repairs and damage control under time pressure.

items.item: Vice/Mole grips, hacksaw + blades, files, hand saw, oscillating multitool
items.why: Cut/grip/shape when nothing fits.

items.item: Hand-operated rivet gun + assorted rivets
items.why: Aluminium and hardware repairs without power.

items.item: Dive kit: mask/fins, weight, spare line cutter, hookah or full tank
items.why: Clear a fouled prop, inspect the keel/rudder, free a snagged anchor — common and unavoidable.

items.item: Headlamps (red/white) + spare batteries; 12V/USB work light
items.why: Night repairs and watch-keeping without ruining night vision.

categories.label: Galley, water & food autonomy
categories.note: Provision for the longest leg + a safety margin; resupply can be poor and expensive in remote ports.

items.item: Long-passage food stores (longest leg + ~50% reserve), shelf-stable + canned + dried
items.why: Pacific legs run 3–4 weeks; carry margin for a slow passage.

items.item: Pressure cooker + non-skid sea-going cookware + gimbaled-stove fiddle
items.why: Fast, fuel-efficient cooking that stays on the stove in a seaway.

items.item: Cooking-fuel supply + adapters (propane/butane) + spare regulator/hose + sniffer
items.why: Fittings vary by country; carry adapters and a gas-leak detector.

items.item: Water treatment: spare filters, purification tablets/drops, UV pen
items.why: Shore water and watermaker backup; giardia/contamination are real in remote stops.

items.item: Vacuum sealer + ample storage; egg/produce preservation supplies
items.why: Extends fresh-food life on long legs.

items.item: Thermos/insulated bottles + jackline-safe mugs
items.why: Hot drinks on cold Patagonian watches without spills.

categories.label: Medical & health
categories.note: Days from definitive care. Carry a real offshore kit and the training to use it.

items.item: Offshore medical kit (trauma + Rx) to a recognised standard, custom-stocked
items.why: Suturing, fracture, burns, and serious-infection capability — not a coastal first-aid box.
items.spec: Build with a doctor; include controlled meds with a prescription letter.

items.item: Prescription antibiotics (broad spectrum), strong analgesics, antiemetics
items.why: Infection and pain management when evacuation is days away.

items.item: Telemedicine subscription + medical guide (e.g. ship's medical guide)
items.why: Expert guidance over sat comms turns a crisis into a managed problem.

items.item: Seasickness meds (multiple types incl. suppository/patch)
items.why: An incapacitated half of a 2-person crew is a safety emergency.

items.item: Personal Rx (months of supply) + spare glasses + dental emergency kit
items.why: No pharmacies mid-ocean; a lost filling/abscess is debilitating.

items.item: Sun & cold injury care: high-SPF, zinc, lip balm, aloe; plus frostnip/hypothermia supplies
items.why: Both climate extremes have their own injury profile.

categories.label: Dog crew (2 dogs) — often-ignored essentials
categories.note: Two dogs add real safety, hygiene, medical, and admin load. This is bespoke to this mission.

items.item: Canine PFDs with lift handle — 1 per dog + spare
items.why: A dog overboard, short-handed, in cold water is recoverable only with a handle and flotation.

items.item: Dog harness + secure cockpit tether/clip points
items.why: Keep dogs attached on deck in a seaway, like the human crew.

items.item: Boarding aid: ramp/steps/sling for dog + human MOB-style hoist
items.why: Dinghy and MOB re-boarding for animals that can't climb a transom.

items.item: Onboard toileting solution (artificial-turf pad + securing + rinse system)
items.why: Multi-week passages with no shore stops; train before departure.

items.item: Dog food stockpile (longest leg + reserve) + sealed storage + bowls (non-skid)
items.why: Brand availability is poor abroad; sudden diet changes cause GI illness at sea.

items.item: Canine medical: vet first-aid kit, parasite/tick/heartworm meds, prescriptions, sedation (vet-advised)
items.why: Vet care is unavailable mid-ocean; storms may require calming.

items.item: Import paperwork: microchip, rabies titer (FAVN), vaccination records, health certificates per country
items.why: Pacific nations (and Chile/NZ/Australia) have strict, slow, non-negotiable pet-import rules; getting this wrong can mean quarantine or refusal.
items.spec: Plan FAVN titer timing MONTHS ahead — it's the long-lead admin item.

items.item: Cold + heat management: dog coats/booties (Patagonia), cooling mats + shade + fresh water (tropics)
items.why: Dogs handle both extremes worse than the humans; paws burn on hot decks, hypothermia in cold.

categories.label: Crew clothing & comfort (two climates)
categories.note: One wardrobe must cover sub-freezing Southern Ocean watches and equatorial heat.

items.item: Offshore foul-weather gear (ocean-grade) + thermal base/mid layers + sea boots + gloves
items.why: Wet + cold + windchill on watch is a hypothermia and decision-quality risk.

items.item: Cold-weather extras: hats, neck gaiters, hand warmers, spare dry gloves
items.why: Patagonia watches are brutal; dry hands keep you safe on deck.

items.item: Tropical sun protection: UPF clothing, wide-brim hats, polarized sunglasses, reef-safe sunblock
items.why: Equatorial UV exposure over weeks causes real harm.

items.item: Lee-cloths/bunk straps + good offshore bedding
items.why: Sleep is a safety system for a 2-person watch rotation.

items.item: Personal dry bags + quick-dry towels
items.why: Keeping kit dry is morale and health.

categories.label: Tender & shore logistics
categories.note: Your car, truck, and lifeboat-of-convenience in remote anchorages.

items.item: Dinghy + reliable outboard + oars + repair kit + spare prop/shear pins
items.why: Only way ashore in 99% of remote anchorages; must be self-repairable.

items.item: Dinghy anchor + long painter + wheels + lifting bridle
items.why: Land on surf beaches, secure against tide, hoist aboard for passages.

items.item: Portable fuel + siphon + spare outboard spark plugs/impeller
items.why: Outboards foul and overheat; the wear parts are cheap and tiny.

items.item: Folding cart + heavy-duty jerry/water jugs
items.why: Haul fuel, water, and provisions long distances from remote landings.

categories.label: Documentation, admin & money
categories.note: Bureaucracy is a passage hazard; missing papers strand you.

items.item: Vessel papers: registration/documentation, radio licence, insurance (offshore + the right cruising grounds), proof of ownership
items.why: Required at every clearance; insurance must explicitly cover high latitudes and ocean passages.

items.item: Crew docs: passports (6+ mo validity), visas, vaccination records, crew list
items.why: Entry refusal for a short-handed couple is a real risk without correct paperwork.

items.item: Courtesy + Q flags for the route; clearance/zarpe folder
items.why: Required on arrival; a zarpe (departure clearance) is demanded by the next port.

items.item: Cash reserve (USD + local) + multiple cards + offline copies of all documents
items.why: ATMs and card networks fail in remote ports; clearance fees are often cash-only.

items.item: Float plan + emergency contacts + offshore SAR coordination info
items.why: Someone ashore must know your plan and who to call.
